name: Catalan

ancestors.notes: Central Catalan continues Old Catalan directly; the substrate/superstrate/adstrate layers (Iberian, Gothic, Andalusi Arabic, Occitan) are declared on the Old-Catalan node.

allophone_rules.notes: Word-final cluster simplification (Wheeler 2005 §10.4; Recasens 1993; Veny 1982 ch. 3): an ABSOLUTELY word-final stop is DELETED after a homorganic nasal or lateral — camp [ˈkam], sang [ˈsaŋ], quant [ˈkwan], molt [ˈmol]. An innovation of CENTRAL, NORTH-WESTERN and NORTHERN Catalan against the Old-Catalan core, which keeps the stop; VALENCIAN and BALEARIC keep it too (camp [ˈkamp], molt [ˈmolt]), and the 4catac expert transcriptions confirm the split on the one token that is diagnostic because a vowel follows — molt alta = Central/North-Western [ˈmol ˈalt-] vs Valencian/Balearic [ˈmolt ˈalt-]. The isogloss is therefore Central + North-Western (+ Northern) against Valencian + Balearic; it is NOT the Eastern/Western line. Overrides the core's conservative CA_CLUSTER_*_FINAL rule by id.

Word-final cluster simplification (Wheeler 2005 §10.4; Recasens 1993; Veny 1982 ch. 3): an ABSOLUTELY word-final stop is DELETED after a homorganic nasal or lateral — camp [ˈkam], sang [ˈsaŋ], quant [ˈkwan], molt [ˈmol]. An innovation of CENTRAL, NORTH-WESTERN and NORTHERN Catalan against the Old-Catalan core, which keeps the stop; VALENCIAN and BALEARIC keep it too (camp [ˈkamp], molt [ˈmolt]), and the 4catac expert transcriptions confirm the split on the one token that is diagnostic because a vowel follows — molt alta = Central/North-Western [ˈmol ˈalt-] vs Valencian/Balearic [ˈmolt ˈalt-]. The isogloss is therefore Central + North-Western (+ Northern) against Valencian + Balearic; it is NOT the Eastern/Western line. Overrides the core's conservative CA_CLUSTER_*_FINAL rule by id.

Word-final cluster simplification (Wheeler 2005 §10.4; Recasens 1993; Veny 1982 ch. 3): an ABSOLUTELY word-final stop is DELETED after a homorganic nasal or lateral — camp [ˈkam], sang [ˈsaŋ], quant [ˈkwan], molt [ˈmol]. An innovation of CENTRAL, NORTH-WESTERN and NORTHERN Catalan against the Old-Catalan core, which keeps the stop; VALENCIAN and BALEARIC keep it too (camp [ˈkamp], molt [ˈmolt]), and the 4catac expert transcriptions confirm the split on the one token that is diagnostic because a vowel follows — molt alta = Central/North-Western [ˈmol ˈalt-] vs Valencian/Balearic [ˈmolt ˈalt-]. The isogloss is therefore Central + North-Western (+ Northern) against Valencian + Balearic; it is NOT the Eastern/Western line. Overrides the core's conservative CA_CLUSTER_*_FINAL rule by id.

Central Catalan defaults an unmarked stressed ⟨o⟩ to open-mid [ɔ] and lists the close-[o] lexemes (the DCVB-cited word_exceptions gos/tot/dos/molt). 'corre' (córrer, close [ˈkorə]) was missing from that list, so this word-keyed rule pins ONLY its stressed vowel to close [o] and lets reduction and spirantisation run normally (DCVB s.v. córrer; Recasens 1996; the ca-009 arbitration).

stress.notes: Catalan accentuation per the Institut d'Estudis Catalans (IEC) norm (Gramàtica de la llengua catalana, ch. 3; Wheeler 2005 §3.1). A written accent (à è é í ò ó ú) always wins and marks the stressed vowel. Unmarked words are OXYTONE when they end in a consonant other than -s-preceded-by-a-vowel and other than -en/-in — including the consonant+s plurals (-ns, -rs, -ts, -nts, -ps …), which are oxytone because the plural preserves the stress of the singular (important → importants). A word ending in a falling DIPHTHONG (-ai -au -ei -eu -iu -oi -ou) is oxytone (remei [rəˈmɛj], correu [kuˈrɛw], avui [əˈβuj]) — the diphthong is one syllable, so the word does not end in a bare vowel. Unmarked words are PAROXYTONE when they end in a (single) vowel, in a vowel + -s (-as, -es, -is, -os, -us) or in -en/-in (semblen, parlin). The diaeresis ⟨ï ü⟩ marks hiatus/pronounced ⟨u⟩, NOT stress, so it is deliberately absent from marked_vowels (següent is [səˈɣwen], not [səˈɣwent] with stress on ⟨ü⟩). Proparoxytones always carry a written accent, so the unmarked default is paroxytone. The diphthongs list is the Catalan inventory of tautosyllabic vowel sequences (Wheeler 2005 §2.5; IEC grammar ch. 4): the falling diphthongs ⟨ai au ei eu iu oi ou ui⟩ and the rising ⟨ua ue uo ui⟩ after ⟨g/q⟩. Every other vowel sequence is HIATUS and counts as two syllables — te-ni-a, ve-ï-na, di-a — which is what puts the stress, and therefore the unstressed-vowel reduction, on the right vowel. CLITICS: cliticless_words lists the Catalan mots atons (IEC Gramatica de la llengua catalana ch. 3 and 6.2; Wheeler 2005 3.1) — definite articles el/la/els/les, the salat/literary lo/los, indefinite un/una/uns/unes, the article-preposition contractions al/als/del/dels/pel/pels, personal articles can/cal, the atonic prepositions a/de/en/amb/per/des, the conjunctions i/o/ni/que and atonic com, and the weak pronouns em/et/es/ens/us/el/la/els/les/li/hi/ho/ne and their reduced shapes. A clitic given a reduced lexical form in word_exceptions must not then be re-stressed (Recasens 1996).

notes: CENTRAL CATALAN (the Barcelona standard codified by the Institut d'Estudis Catalans). ⟨ca⟩ is the standard language and remains the code for 'Catalan'; what it is NOT is the parent of the other varieties — they inherit from ca-x-medieval (Old Catalan), so nothing declared here can leak into them. Everything in this file is a CENTRAL innovation against the Old-Catalan core: (1) stress-conditioned unstressed vowel reduction — unstressed /a e ɛ/ → [ə] and /o ɔ/ → [u], STRESSED vowels keeping full quality (gos [ˈɡos], dona [ˈdɔnə]) — keyed to the ⟨nucleus_unstressed⟩ slot, not to every nucleus (Wheeler 2005 §2.3); (2) betacism, ⟨v⟩ merged into /b/; (3) the fricative [ʒ] for ⟨j⟩ and ⟨g⟩ before a front vowel, where Old Catalan (and Valencian, and North-Western) has the affricate [dʒ]; (4) ⟨ix⟩ = [ʃ], the glide absorbed; (5) deletion of word-final ⟨-r⟩ (cantar [kənˈta]); and (6) deletion of an absolutely word-final stop in an ⟨-nt/-lt/-mp/-nc⟩ cluster (camp [ˈkam], molt [ˈmol]) — an innovation shared with North-Western and Northern but NOT with Valencian or Balearic. The stressed ⟨e⟩/⟨o⟩ close/open contrast is NOT recoverable from Catalan spelling (gos [ˈɡos] vs cos [ˈkɔs]); the default prior is OPEN-mid first, and the close-mid words are listed in word_exceptions.

sources.author: Wheeler, M. W.
sources.title: The Phonology of Catalan
sources.notes: The reference phonology of Catalan and the primary authority for this spec. Cited by SECTION, not page: §2.3 vowel systems and the unstressed reduction of the Eastern block (reduction is stress-conditioned; the Western block does not reduce); §2.5 the diphthong inventory; §3.1 stress and its orthographic marking; §5.1 the consonant inventory, ⟨c/g⟩ before a front vowel, ⟨x⟩ and word-final ⟨-ig⟩; §5.2 spirantization of /b d ɡ/ after a continuant and the pre-lateral gemination that blocks it; §5.3 final-obstruent devoicing and cross-word voicing assimilation; §10.2–§10.5 the post-lexical/phrase-level processes — final-rhotic loss, final-cluster simplification, nasal place assimilation and phrase-level lenition. Section numbers are given because the printed page numbers were not verified.

sources.author: Badia i Margarit, A. M.
sources.title: Gramàtica de la llengua catalana: descriptiva, normativa, diatòpica, diacrònica
sources.notes: Descriptive, normative, diatopic and diachronic grammar of Catalan; the cross-check used here for the consonant and vowel inventories, the stress rules and the dialect isoglosses. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Recasens, D.
sources.title: Fonètica i fonologia
sources.notes: Descriptive phonetics of Central Catalan: nasal place assimilation (labial before /p b m f/, velar before /k ɡ/, palato-alveolar before /ʃ ʒ tʃ dʒ ʎ/), spirantization and final-obstruent devoicing. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Recasens, D.
sources.title: Fonètica descriptiva del català: assaig de caracterització de la pronúncia del vocalisme i consonantisme del català al segle XX
sources.notes: The descriptive survey of Catalan vowels and consonants across the dialects: the Western block (Valencian, North-Western) does not reduce unstressed vowels; Balearic reduces ⟨a⟩/⟨e⟩ to [ə] but does NOT raise unstressed ⟨o⟩ to [u]; the North-Western word-final ⟨-a⟩ = [ɛ]. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Institut d'Estudis Catalans
sources.title: Gramàtica de la llengua catalana
sources.notes: The normative grammar. Ch. 3 (l'accent) is the source for the stress block: the written accent marks the stressed vowel and always wins; unmarked words ending in a vowel, a vowel+⟨-s⟩ or ⟨-en/-in⟩ are paroxytone and all other endings — including the consonant+⟨s⟩ plurals — are oxytone; the diaeresis ⟨ï ü⟩ marks hiatus, not stress. §6.2 and the 'mots àtons' list are the source for the clitic function words (articles, contractions, atonic prepositions/conjunctions and weak pronouns) that carry no stress and therefore reduce. Cited by chapter; pages not verified.

sources.author: Veny, J.
sources.title: Els parlars catalans: síntesi de dialectologia
sources.notes: The standard dialectology of Catalan and the source for the isoglosses this spec encodes per dialect: final ⟨-r⟩ retained in Valencian and deleted in the rest; final-cluster simplification after a nasal everywhere but after a lateral only in the Eastern block; Western [tʃ] for initial ⟨x⟩ and [jʃ] for ⟨ix⟩; the lleidatà final ⟨-a⟩ = [ɛ]. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Alcover, A. M. & Moll, F. de B.
sources.title: Diccionari català-valencià-balear
sources.notes: The dialectal dictionary of Catalan; it records the pronunciation of each headword per dialect and is the authority for the lexical stressed-mid-vowel entries in word_exceptions (gos, tot, dos, molt are close-mid [o]; cos, pont, font, tres are open-mid). Catalan spelling does not mark the ⟨e⟩/⟨o⟩ close/open contrast in unaccented syllables, so this contrast is lexical and can only be listed, not derived. Cited by headword; the dictionary is not paginated online.

sources.author: Wiktionary contributors
sources.title: Wiktionary, the free dictionary — Catalan entries
sources.notes: Per-dialect IPA transcriptions (Central / Valencian / North-Western / Balearic / Northern) for the individual headwords listed in word_exceptions; each entry there cites GDLC, DNV or DCVB. Used as a convenience cross-check on the DCVB, never as a primary source.

sources.author: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I.
sources.title: The Sounds of the World's Languages
sources.notes: Cross-linguistic phonetic reference for the segment inventory symbols used here.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Catalan phonology (Wikipedia)
sources.notes: Accessible overview of the vowel and consonant inventories, the Eastern/Western reduction split, spirantization, final devoicing and final-cluster simplification; useful as a cross-check on the primary sources above.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Catalan orthography (Wikipedia)
sources.notes: Accessible overview of the grapheme→sound correspondences encoded in the graphemes table: ⟨ll⟩, ⟨ny⟩, ⟨l·l⟩, ⟨tx⟩, ⟨tg/tj⟩, ⟨ix⟩, word-final ⟨-ig⟩, ⟨ç⟩, ⟨qu/gu⟩ and the diaeresis.
